retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Excisional lymph node histology demonstrates diffuse effacement of nodal architecture along with some scattered residual likely germinal centers. These lymph node infiltrates are predominantly composed of small lymphocytes. However, large lymphoid cells, such as pro-lymphocytes, are always present in clusters and are known as " pseudo-follicles" (proliferation centers), a pathognomic finding, and CLL/SLL patients. Spleen histology demonstrates the infiltration of red and white pulp with a more prominent white pulp involvement compared to red pulp. From an imaging standpoint, a CT scan helps in evaluation to see the degree of lymphadenopathy and organ infiltration in the form of spleen and liver sizes. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Lymph Node Dissection -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic system, or lymphoid system, is a part of the circulatory system and the immune system. It consists of the primary lymphoid organs and the secondary lymphoid organs. The thymus and the bone marrow constitute the primary lymphoid organs. Both of them are involved in the production and early clonal selection of lymphocytes. Bone marrow is responsible for the production and maturation of B cells and the production of T cells. B cells travel from the bone marrow to the secondary lymphoid organs in search of pathogens. T cells, on the other hand, travel from the bone marrow to the thymus, where their maturation takes place. The thymus, as mentioned earlier, is a primary lymphoid organ. It provides an environment for the development and maturation of T cells. In addition, one of the most important roles of the thymus is the induction of central tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The normal architecture of lymph nodes is distorted and is replaced by sheets of atypical lymphoid cells with large nuclei, basophilic cytoplasm, and high proliferation rate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Adenoids -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: As a portion of the Waldeyer ring, adenoids compose the lymphoid tissue that serves as a defense against potential pathogens in the pharynx. Adenoids, in conjunction with the lingual and palatine tonsils, are involved in the development of T cells and B cells. On the surface, adenoid tissue has specialized antigen-capture cells (ACC), M cells, which uptake the pathogenic antigens and then alert the underlying B cells. Activation of B cells leads their proliferation in areas called germinal centers; this helps in producing IgA immunoglobulins. Through this mechanism, the adenoids aid in the development of immunologic memory throughout childhood. Recent scientific literature has provided some evidence that adenoids also produce T lymphocytes (cellular immunity) like the thymus gland. The adenoids can function as a bacterial reservoir for the nasal cavity and are implicated in the pathogenesis of chronic rhinosinusitis. [1] [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoblastic Lymphoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of B- and T-cell LBL is different. T-LBL commonly involves the mediastinum (thymus); other possible sites include skin, tonsils, and spleen. Since T-LBL is more common than T-ALL, presentation with mediastinal masses with rapid growth is common. [1] T-lymphoblasts cannot be differentiated from B-lymphoblasts based on morphology, immunohistochemistry (IHC0, and flow cytometry are essential to render a diagnosis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Mediastinal Mass -- Evaluation -- Tissue Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Flow cytometry: Flow cytometric analysis of tissue specimens, particularly in cases of lymphoproliferative disorders, can provide valuable information about the immunophenotypic characteristics of the cells comprising the mass. Flow cytometry helps identify cell surface markers, immunoglobulin or T-cell receptor gene rearrangements, and clonal populations of lymphoid cells, aiding in classifying and subtyping lymphomas and other hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Bruton Agammaglobulinemia (Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to lack of B cell maturation, differnciation, and storage, lymphoid organs like the tonsils, spleen, adenoids, Peyer patches in the intestines, are poorly developed. The lymph nodes can, however, appear normal due to hypertrophy of T-cell areas.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Lymphatic System -- Introduction -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Several organs in the body are considered to be lymphoid or lymphatic organs, given their role in the production of lymphocytes. These include the bone marrow, spleen, thymus, tonsils, lymph nodes, and other tissues. Lymphoid organs can be categorized as primary or secondary lymphoid organs. Primary lymphoid organs are those that produce lymphocytes, such as the bone marrow and thymus. Bone marrow is the primary site for the production of lymphocytes. The thymus is a glandular organ located anterior to the pericardium. It serves to mature and develop T cells, or thymus cell lymphocytes, in response to an inflammatory process or pathology. As individuals age, both their bone marrow and thymus reduce and accumulate fat. Secondary lymphoid organs serve as territories in which immune cells function and include the spleen, tonsils, lymph nodes, and various mucous membranes, such as in the intestines. The spleen is a purplish, fist-sized organ in the left upper abdominal quadrant that contributes to immune function by serving as a blood filter, storing lymphocytes within its white pulp, and being a site for an adaptive immune response to antigens. The lingual tonsils, palatine tonsils, and pharyngeal tonsils, or adenoids, work to prevent pathogens from entering the body. Mucous membranes in the gastrointestinal, respiratory, and genitourinary systems also function to prevent pathogens from entering the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, B-Cell Lymphocyte -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: B cells are predominantly found in the cortex of lymph nodes and form part of the B-cell follicles, which can be classified into 2 types. Primary follicles are small and uniform, containing naive or resting B cells. In contrast, secondary follicles are characterized by the presence of a germinal center, where B cells undergo activation, proliferation, and differentiation into plasma cells or memory B cells. [6] The germinal center consists of a light zone containing centrocytes and a dark zone containing centroblasts. [7] In the spleen, B cells are located in the white pulp, specifically in periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths and follicles, similar to those observed in lymph nodes. [8] In addition, B cells are prominent in mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, including in the tonsils and Peyer's patches, where they also form germinal centers. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoma -- Staging -- Stage I
retrieved_docs.content: Involvement of a single lymph node region or lymphoid structure (e.g., spleen, thymus, Waldeyer’s ring) or involvement of a single extra lymphatic site (IE)

retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Evaluation -- Lymphoproliferation
retrieved_docs.content: Sonography, CT, or magnetic resonance (MR) of the abdomen (on-demand and in case of suspected lymphoma) Lymph node biopsy (on-demand and in case of suspected lymphoma)

retrieved_docs.title: Adenopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic fluid moves throughout the lymphatic system, transitioning from the organs to lymphoid capillaries, lymphatic vessels, and finally lymph nodes for foreign antigen filtration. Foreign substances are presented to the lymphoid cells, which lead to cellular proliferation and enlargement. Under microscopy, cellular proliferation in lymphoid follicles may be identified as several mitotic figures. [5] As lymphatic capsules stretch due to increased activity, patients may experience localized tenderness.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Lymph Nodes -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: The lymph node capsule is thin fibrous, which receives afferent lymphatics. The naïve lymph node has not received any antigenic stimulation and hence, has all primary lymphoid follicles lacking a germinal center. In response to antigens, it transforms into secondary lymphoid follicles with germinal centers and a peripheral mantle zone with small mature B cells. The germinal center is composed of centroblasts, centrocytes, tingible body macrophages, and follicular dendritic cells. In between the capsule and the cortex are the subcapsular sinuses that are the primary site of metastatic carcinomas. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Castleman Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The hyaline vascular type has an increased number of lymphoid follicles with a broad mantle zone and hypoplastic germinal center. Centrally placed germinal center is surrounded with small lymphocytes arranged in concentric circles, forming an "onion skin-like" structure. The center of these follicles is penetrated by hyalinized capillaries resembling a "lollipop" (also known as the lollipop sign). Strong endothelial vascular proliferation and hyalinization in the interfollicular zones along with a varying number of small lymphocytes and immature plasma cells, and prominent follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) occasionally appearing dysplastic can be seen. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunodeficiency -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphoid atrophy is a prominent morphological feature of malnutrition. Histologically, the lobular architecture is ill-defined, there is a loss of corticomedullary demarcation, and there are fewer lymphoid cells. Hassall's corpuscles are enlarged and degenerate; some may be calcified. Atrophy is observed in the thymus-dependent periarteriolar areas of the spleen and the paracortical section of the lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Mucosa-Associated Lymphoma Tissue (Archived) -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Lugano staging system for the gastrointestinal tract lymphoma: Stage I: Confined to the GI tract (single primary or multiple, non-contiguous) Stage II: Extending into the abdomen     II1: Local nodal involvement     II2: Distant nodal involvement Stage IIE: Penetration of serosa to involve adjacent organs or tissues Stage IV: Disseminated extra-nodal involvement or concomitant supra-diaphragmatic nodal involvement

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2231
retrieved_docs.content: The hematopoietic and lymphatic system is identified as the anatomic site associated with metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, indicating that involvement of this system is typical in such metastatic spread. In addition, the presence of a metastatic lesion is recognized as a disease‑finding of metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, underscoring its diagnostic importance. Together, these relationships emphasize the need to evaluate the hematopoietic and lymphatic structures and to identify metastatic lesions when assessing patients suspected of having supraclavicular lymph node metastasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled growth of cancerous cells. The thyroid‑originating tumor that spreads to cervical lymph nodes illustrates how a primary malignant neoplasm can metastasize, while the primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus exemplifies a malignant proliferation arising directly within the thymic tissue. Both conditions underscore the importance of recognizing malignant neoplastic proliferations as pathological processes that require accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is identified as a clinical finding that indicates the presence of a Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the thymus, reflecting the relationship “is_finding_of_disease.” The Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the thymus is further linked to the entity النقيلة, denoting a related thymic mass or nodule. Together, these concepts describe how the detection of a metastatic lesion serves as evidence of a malignant metastatic tumor involving the thymus, which is commonly associated with a palpable or radiographic thymic mass. Recognizing this association aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing metastatic disease affecting the thymus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: سرطان العقد اللمفاوية (classified_as) ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, indicating that lymph node cancer is a type of lymph gland tumor. ورم الغدد الليمفاوية is a child of Neoplasm of lymph node, showing that this tumor is a specific subclass within the broader category of lymph node neoplasms. The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is the associated anatomic site of ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, reflecting that these tumors arise in the tissues of the blood‑forming and lymphatic systems. Together, these relationships define lymph node cancer as a lymph gland tumor that belongs to the category of lymph node neoplasms and is localized to the hematopoietic and lymphatic system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1130
retrieved_docs.content: Lymph node cancer (سرطان العقد اللمفاوية) is classified as a lymph gland tumor (ورم الغدد الليمفاوية), and this tumor type is situated within the broader category of malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites. Within the hierarchical structure, the neoplasm of lymph node (Neoplasm of lymph node) lists the lymph gland tumor as a child concept, indicating that the tumor is a specific form of lymph‑node neoplasm. The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is identified as the anatomic site associated with the lymph gland tumor, underscoring the relevance of this system in the disease’s location. The concept of spread to lymph nodes (انتشار إلى العقد اللمفاوية) functions as a parent category for malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites and is linked inversely to both the neoplasm of lymph node and lymph node cancer, reflecting that these neoplastic entities represent particular manifestations of disease dissemination to lymph nodes. Together, these relationships delineate how lymph node cancer, lymph gland tumor, and related neoplasms are organized within broader oncologic classifications and anatomical contexts, aiding clinical understanding of disease categorization and spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1236
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites is a broader classification that encompasses the condition referred to in Arabic as ورم الغدد الليمفاوية (lymph gland tumor). Within the hierarchy of lymphatic neoplasms, Neoplasm of lymph node is a parent entity that has ورم الغدد الليمفاوية as a child, indicating that the lymph gland tumor is a specific type of lymph node neoplasm. The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is identified as the anatomic site associated with ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, reflecting that this tumor arises in tissues of the blood‑forming and lymphatic systems. Together, these relationships clarify that the lymph gland tumor is a specific malignant neoplasm of the lymph nodes, situated within the broader category of malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites, and localized to the hematopoietic and lymphatic system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2633
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes is directly associated with the lymphatic system, specifically the lymph nodes (lymfatické uzliny).
